name: django-remake-migrations

metadata.name: django-remake-migrations
metadata.summary: A Django admin command to recreate all migrations in a project.

documentation:
# Django remake migrations

--- **Documentation**: https://django-remake-migrations.readthedocs.io **Source Code**: https://github.com/browniebroke/django-remake-migrations --- A Django admin command to recreate all migrations in a project. Like a `squashmigrations` command on steroids. ## The problem The built-in `squashmigrations` command is great, but it has some limitations: - **It only works on a single app at a time**, which means that you need to run it for each app in your project. On a project with enough cross-apps dependencies, it quickly becomes impossible to run. - **It doesn't optimise the operations**, it only reduces the number of migration files. That being said, Django 4.1 introduced a new [`optimizemigrations` command](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/stable/ref/django-admin/#optimizemigration) which sounds like it might be doing just this. This command aims at solving this problem, by recreating all the migration files in the whole project, from scratch, and mark them as applied by using the `replaces` attribute. It makes an important trade-off though: it does NOT try to be correct when setting the `replaces` attribute. The only guarantees are that: - all old migrations are marked as replaced **once**. - all new migrations replace at least one of the old migrations This is OK to make this trade-off as long as all your environments are fully migrated when you deploy the remade migrations. ## Contributors ✨ Thanks goes to these wonderful people ([emoji key](https://allcontributors.org/docs/en/emoji-key)):

Bruno Alla
💻 🤔 📖
Dmytro Litvinov
📖
This project follows the [all-contributors](https://github.com/all-contributors/all-contributors) specification. Contributions of any kind welcome! ## Credits This package was created with [Copier](https://copier.readthedocs.io/) and the [browniebroke/pypackage-template](https://github.com/browniebroke/pypackage-template) project template.

classes.name: django_remake_migrations.apps.RemakeMigrationAppConfig
documentation: App config for the remake migration app.

modules.name: django_remake_migrations.conf
documentation:
These are the available settings.

All attributes prefixed `REMAKE_MIGRATIONS_*` can be overridden from your Django
project's settings module by defining a setting with the same name.

classes.name: django_remake_migrations.conf.AppSettings

methods.name: django_remake_migrations.conf.AppSettings.__getattribute__
documentation:
Check if a Django project settings should override the app default.

In order to avoid returning any random properties of the django settings,
we inspect the prefix firstly.

class_variables.name: django_remake_migrations.conf.AppSettings.REMAKE_MIGRATIONS_FIRST_APPS
documentation: The apps for which to make migrations first.

class_variables.name: django_remake_migrations.conf.AppSettings.REMAKE_MIGRATIONS_LAST_APPS
documentation: The apps for which to make migrations last.

class_variables.name: django_remake_migrations.conf.AppSettings.REMAKE_MIGRATIONS_POST_COMMANDS
documentation:
Django management commands to run after generating the new migration.

Each command and its arguments should be specified as a list or tuple.
For example, to integrate with django-linear-migrations:

```python
REMAKE_MIGRATIONS_POST_COMMANDS = [
    ["create_max_migration_files", "--recreate"],
]
```

class_variables.name: django_remake_migrations.conf.AppSettings.REMAKE_MIGRATIONS_EXTENSIONS
documentation:
The database extensions to enable for each app.

Keys are app labels and values are a list of the full import path
of the extension classes:

```python
REMAKE_MIGRATIONS_EXTENSIONS = {
    "app1": ["django.contrib.postgres.operations.TrigramExtension"],
}
```

Proxy class to encapsulate all the app settings.

This instance should be accessed via the singleton
`django_remake_migrations.conf.app_settings`.

You shouldn't have to set any of these yourself, the class checks a Django
settings with the same name and use these if defined, defaulting to the
values documented here.

classes.name: django_remake_migrations.management.commands.remakemigrations.Command

methods.name: django_remake_migrations.management.commands.remakemigrations.Command.handle
documentation: Execute one step after another to avoid side effects between steps.

documentation: Wrapper to help logging successes.

methods.name: django_remake_migrations.management.commands.remakemigrations.Command.log_error
documentation: Wrapper to help logging errors.

methods.name: django_remake_migrations.management.commands.remakemigrations.Command.clear_old_migrations
documentation: Remove all pre-existing migration files in first party apps.

methods.name: django_remake_migrations.management.commands.remakemigrations.Command.make_migrations
documentation: Recreate migrations from scratch with a unique name.

methods.name: django_remake_migrations.management.commands.remakemigrations.Command.remove_migration_file
documentation: Remove file from the disk for the specified migration.

methods.name: django_remake_migrations.management.commands.remakemigrations.Command.update_new_migrations
documentation:
Update auto-generated migrations after Django re-created them.

Does a lot of things:

- Rename migration files to have a unique name. If they have the same name as
  an old one, Django may be confused with replaces or dependencies.
- Update dependencies to use the new unique names.
- Add the old migrations to the `replaces` attributes of the new migrations.
  This is to mark the new migrations as squashed, so they are not actually
  executed by Django, they are simply marked as already applied.

methods.name: django_remake_migrations.management.commands.remakemigrations.Command.sort_migrations_map
documentation: Sort migrations and group them by app.

methods.name: django_remake_migrations.management.commands.remakemigrations.Command.add_needed_database_extensions
documentation: Add DB extensions for the app to the migration file.

methods.name: django_remake_migrations.management.commands.remakemigrations.Command.write_to_disk
documentation: Write the migration object to the disk.

methods.name: django_remake_migrations.management.commands.remakemigrations.Command.run_post_commands
documentation: Run other management commands at the very end.

Command to recreate all migrations from scratch.

Mark new migrations as squashed.

This might cause some inconsistencies, but should get you 99% there,
after which we can fix issues manually.

Can be validated by running:
- showmigrations: should show the migration graph as expected
- migrate: should not execute any migration
- makemigrations: should not detect any differences
